title: First-Order Transitions in Weak Ising Spin-Orbit-Coupled Superconductors
authors: Xusheng Wang, Gaomin Tang, Shuai-hua Ji
abstract: Ising spin-orbit coupling (ISOC) can strongly protect superconductivity against exchange-field-induced depairing, typically leading to critical fields far exceeding the Pauli limit and continuous (second-order) phase transitions. Here, using a free-energy approach, we demonstrate that first-order transitions can emerge in superconductors with weak ISOC under large exchange fields. In this regime, conventional theoretical approaches based on the gap equation fail to determine the thermodynamic critical field and instead yield only the supercooling field. Moreover, we identify two pronounced in-gap coherence peaks in the quasiparticle spectra, which represent the weak-ISOC manifestation of the previously reported mirage-gap states. Our results establish the importance of free-energy analysis in describing the first-order phase transitions in Ising superconductors and reveal distinct spectroscopic signatures of the weak-ISOC regime.
summary: This paper shows that superconductors with weak Ising spin-orbit coupling can undergo first-order phase transitions under high magnetic fields, rather than the typical continuous transitions. It also predicts unique spectroscopic signatures in the density of states that could be used to identify this regime experimentally.
main_problem: Investigating the emergence of first-order phase transitions and the nature of in-gap mirage-gap states in superconductors with weak Ising spin-orbit coupling under large exchange fields.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that weak Ising spin-orbit coupling allows for first-order transitions under large exchange fields and identify distinct spectroscopic signatures, specifically pronounced in-gap coherence peaks.
method: A free-energy analysis approach is used to determine thermodynamic critical fields, complemented by Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) formalism to calculate quasiparticle spectra and density of states.
model_or_system: The study focuses on 2D s-wave spin-singlet superconductors with Ising spin-orbit coupling (ISOC), such as transition metal dichalcogenide (TMD) monolayers, subject to an in-plane magnetic field.
key_observables: Superconducting gap (delta), thermodynamic critical field, supercooling field, density of states (DOS), and in-gap coherence peaks.
important_parameters: Ising spin-orbit coupling strength (beta_so), exchange field (h), reduced temperature (t), and the Pauli limit.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a 2D s-wave spin-singlet pairing symmetry and uses a cutoff energy approximation (epsilon_c >> delta_0).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the ISOC schematic, phase diagrams, and free-energy landscapes; Figure 3 displays pseudocolor maps and spectra of the superconducting DOS evolution under increasing magnetic fields.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of phase transitions in ISOC superconductors, presents the theoretical framework (Hamiltonian and free-energy derivation), analyzes the phase diagrams and transition types across different ISOC strengths, investigates the spectroscopic signatures in the DOS, and concludes with implications for experimental realization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of ISOC and the distinction between FOTs and SOTs in the absence of ISOC. (a) Schematic band structure of the normal state with ISOC. K and −K denote the two valleys, and µ is the chemical potential. (b) Phase diagram in the zero-ISOC limit. The solid yellow curve denotes the FOT critical field obtained from free-energy analysis. The solid blue curve represents the SOT critical field, which can be determined by both the free-energy and gap-equation approaches. The dashed blue curve indicates the supercooling field in the FOT regime, obtained from the gap equation. The red filled diamond marks the critical point separating the FOT and SOT regimes. Bp denotes the...

FIG. 2. Field-temperature (h-t) phase diagrams and superconducting gap behavior for weak Ising superconductors with dif- ferent ISOC strengths. (a-d) Phase diagrams and corresponding ∆(h) curves at representative temperatures (t = 0.02, 0.2, 0.4) for (a) βso = 0.1, (b) βso = 0.35, (c) βso = 0.45, and (d) βso = 0.6. The solid yellow curves denote the FOT critical field hc(t) obtained from free-energy analysis. The solid blue curves represent the SOT critical field. The dashed blue curves indicate the supercooling field in the FOT regime. The densely dashed silver curve in (b) marks the FOT occurring below the critical field. The color scale represents the normalized gap ∆(t, h)/∆0.

FIG. 3. Evolution of the superconducting DOS N(E) under increasing in-plane magnetic field for four representative ISOC strengths at t = 0.02. (a-d) Pseudocolor maps of N(E) as a function of energy and magnetic field, together with representative spectra at selected fields, for (a) βso = 0.1, (b) βso = 0.35, (c) βso = 0.45, and (d) βso = 1.5. Here, ˜h = B/Bp denotes the magnetic field normalized by the Pauli limit. A Dynes broadening parameter Γ = 0.001 (normalized by ∆0) is used throughout. The color scale represents N(E)/N0.

FIG. 4. Superconducting behavior under in-plane magnetic fields in the phase space spanned by βso and t. (a) Critical field hc(t, βso) as a function of βso and t. The color scale represents the normalized critical field hc(t, βso)/hp. Contour lines highlight the enhancement of the critical field due to ISOC. (b) Classification of superconducting behaviors in the (βso, t) phase space. The red region indicates an FOT at the critical field. The orange region denotes an FOT transition occurring below a second-order critical field. The blue region corresponds to an SOT at the critical field. The white region represents the normal state.
